coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot and FITC conjugated dual-emission nanoprobe and its electrospun film for the sensing of ammonia
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Shaoyong
Deng, Mengyu
Zhou, Dongyan
Su, Fengyu
Liu, Yan Jun
Tian, Yanqing
coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials are at the forefront of next-generation sensing technologies because of their unique and tunable properties. For monitoring trace ammonia (NH3) in human metabolism, we synthesized a dual-emission nanoprobe with an average diameter of 4 nm by conjugating the NH3-sensitivity fluorescein derivative, the fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer (FITC, green emitter), onto the surface of carbon dots (CDs, blue emitter). A significant Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect was observed, where the CDs acted as energy donors and FITC moieties acted as energy acceptors. The fluorescent color changed obviously from blue to green once ammonia was introduced into the test chamber. Subsequently, the dual-emission nanoprobe was immobilized in polyvinyl pyrrolidone nanofibers with an average diameter of 400 nm via electrospinning, thus obtaining a ratiometric NH3 sensor. Results showed that the fabricated sensor with a high surface area exhibited high sensitivity (theoretical detection limit was 0.53 ppm), short response time (∼4.3 s), excellent selectivity, and good reversibility in tracing 0–300 ppm NH3. Further prototyped application studies reveal that the sensor is expected to serve as an indicator for monitoring breath/blood ammonia concentration. Thus, the newly developed fluorescent sensor was able to be a feasible solution for the application of nanomaterials in health and medicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoprobe
FRET
Carbon dot
FITC
Electrospinning

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and F-CDs
Fret-based ratiometric nanoprobe determination of ammonia solutions
Sensitivity of the fabricated ratiometric sensors (RNS)
Prototyped applications for the ratiometric sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The DLS detection of CDs in water. Inset: The AFM image and height profile (top right) of the CDs. XPS spectra of CDs and F-CDs: (b) full spectrum, (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s, and (e) S 2p. (f) FTIR spectra of samples.

(a) Emission spectra and (b) response values of F-CDs with different concentrations of ammonia solutions. (c) The CIE chromaticity diagram with emission color coordinates for F-CDs under different conditions. Inset: The fluorescent image of F-CDs aqueous solution (0.1 mg/mL, excitation: 365 nm) during the sensing process and the structure of F-CDs after the formation of the FRET system.

(a) Images of RNS with a blue initial fluorescence. (b) The SEM and fluorescence images of RNS. (c) Emission spectra and (d) response values of RNS at different NH3 concentrations. Inset were the fluorescence images of the sample before and after the test (Excitation: 365 nm). Fluorescence intensity ratios of RNS at different conditions: (e) Response time and (f) cycle detection. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Experimental procedures for the breath analysis. (b) Response characteristics of the nanofiber-based sensor to normal exhaled breath and exhaled breath with additional NH3. (c) Schematic depiction of the designed PAD. (d) The fluorescence images of PAD with pure artificial blood and ammoniated artificial blood (600 μM). (e) Fluorescent color characteristics of F4 vs. the amount of ammonia solution added to artificial blood across a range of 0–600 μM. Inset: The fluorescence images of RNS during the sensing process (Excitation: 365 nm).
The synthesis process of CDs and F-CDs nanoprobes.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot and FITC conjugated dual-emission nanoprobe and its electrospun film for the sensing of ammonia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Academy for Advanced Interdisciplinary Studies, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China (F. Su).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials are at the forefront of next-generation sensing technologies because of their unique and tunable properties. For monitoring trace ammonia (NH3) in human metabolism, we synthesized a dual-emission nanoprobe with an average diameter of 4 nm by conjugating the NH3-sensitivity fluorescein derivative, the fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer (FITC, green emitter), onto the surface of carbon dots (CDs, blue emitter). A significant Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect was observed, where the CDs acted as energy donors and FITC moieties acted as energy acceptors. The fluorescent color changed obviously from blue to green once ammonia was introduced into the test chamber. Subsequently, the dual-emission nanoprobe was immobilized in polyvinyl pyrrolidone nanofibers with an average diameter of 400 nm via electrospinning, thus obtaining a ratiometric NH3 sensor. Results showed that the fabricated sensor with a high surface area exhibited high sensitivity (theoretical detection limit was 0.53 ppm), short response time (∼4.3 s), excellent selectivity, and good reversibility in tracing 0–300 ppm NH3. Further prototyped application studies reveal that the sensor is expected to serve as an indicator for monitoring breath/blood ammonia concentration. Thus, the newly developed fluorescent sensor was able to be a feasible solution for the application of nanomaterials in health and medicine.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ammonia (NH3) is an important analyte in medical diagnosis [1]. Normally, ammonia is generated by human body during the digestion of proteins and transported by the portal vein to the liver and converted into another waste product called urea, which is then excreted through the body in urine [2]. Problems can arise when a person's body is unable to adequately metabolize the produced ammonia, allowing it to accumulate in the bloodstream [3,4]. High ammonia levels (typically level: 11–50 μmol/L or μM) in the blood (ammonemia or hyperammonemia) can lead to serious health problems, including electrolyte abnormalities, brain damage, and even death [5,6]. To date, testing the blood for ammonia involves transporting a cold-stored sample to a lab, then waiting at least two hours as that sample needs to be centrifuged and subjected to a biochemical assay [7]. Undoubtedly, the time-consuming, complex, and highly specialized blood ammonia detection process tends to delay the diagnosis and treatment of hyperammonemia. Recently, there has been a surge of interest in alternative sample matrices for measuring ammonia, particularly in the field of breath ammonia detection. For example, chronic kidney disease and H. pylori infection have been shown to cause increased ammonia concentrations in human breath (typically level: 0.05–2.0 parts per million) [8]. The breath/blood ammonia is measured using different analytical methods, whose main results are summarized in Table S1. However, the relationship between blood ammonia and breath ammonia has not been determined. One of the reasons is the multiple interferences of different testing tools, resulting in the low reliability of the test results using current approaches [9].

Recently, nanomaterials have been regarded as the next-generation sensing technologies because of their unique and tunable properties [10,11]. Compared with other detection methods, including gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GC–MS), and selected ion flow tube mass spectrometry (SIFT-MS), fluorescent nanoprobes have attracted increasing attention due to their quick response, good stability, and ability to support spatially resolved imaging [12–15]. However, a single fluorophore is difficult to quantitatively detect trace ammonia since the fluorescent signal will be affected by the probe concentrations and environmental interferences. This issue can be effectively addressed by the construction of a ratiometric fluorescent system, i.e., the presence of ammonia causes a change in the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths [16,17]. Such internal calbration mechanism can be designed by mechanisms that directly influence the spectral properties (excitation and/or emission) of the fluorescent moiety or, more often, by constructing the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between the donor and acceptor fluorophores [18–20]. FRET based sensors, which are extremely sensitive to distance (<10 nm) between fluorophores, have been widely applied to the analysis of ions, organics, and bioevents [21].

Apart from the distance, the absorbance of the energy acceptor and the fluorescence emission of the energy donor should overlap. Compared with conventional organic chromophores, carbon dots (CDs) with tunable fluorescence emission have become one of the ideal energy donors, which have the advantages of small size, low toxicity, facile surface functionalization, good photostability, and excellent water solubility [22,23]. Immobilization of CDs within the supramolecular framework has been pursued for the past few years with the aim of preventing aggregation-induced fluorescence bursts and producing fluorescent materials for sensing, optical, and other applications. Yang et al. [24] prepared a double-shell fluorescent nanoparticle by sequentially covering the SiO2 core with layers of poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) containing CDs and fluorescent molecules, respectively. The pH-triggered swelling-shrinking of PMAA is utilized to regulate the FRET system in nanoparticles, thus changing the fluorescent color of the sensor. Nevertheless, such sensing mechanism prevented the application of composite nanoparticles for gaseous ammonia detection. So far, the development of an ideal CDs-based dual-emission nanoprobe for ammonia sensing has been quite limited, and more efforts are required.

In this work, we reported a new CDs-based sensing platform for tracing ammonia with low concentrations. CDs (blue emission) served as both an energy donor and an anchoring site for the fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer in the FRET system (FITC, green emission, energy acceptor). The FRET effect in the synthesized FITC-CDs (F-CDs) became significant once ammonia was introduced into the test chamber. Thus, such a dual-emission nanoprobe was capable of color-switchable fluorescence during the sensing process. Furthermore, the synthetic F-CDs nanoprobe could be encapsulated into nanofibers by electrospinning to obtain a ratiometric ammonia sensor with a large specific surface area, excellent sensitivity, and good selectivity. Following prototyped applications, it was demonstrated that the prepared ratiometric sensor could be used as a simple and dependable tool for monitoring trace ammonia in breath and blood, thus providing a feasible solution for the application of nanomaterials in health and medicine.
Reagents used for the synthesis of F-CDs were procured from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ).

The particle size of the sample was characterized by the Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Dimension Edge, Karlsruhe, Germany) and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS, Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, England). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by a Waltham Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer (Waltham, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was examined by a PHI 5000 Versaprobe III (ULVAC-PHI, Kyoto, Japan) with an Al Kα radiational source. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were conducted by a Lambda 950 spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Massachusetts, America) and an RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), respectively. Both the widths of the excitation and emission slits were maintained at 5 nm. The electrospinning process was performed by QZNT-M07 (Qingzi Nano, Guangdong, China).

The CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal reaction, and the experimental steps were shown in Scheme 1
[25]. In detail, 1.00 g anhydrous citric acid and 0.46 g 1,2-ethanediamine were sufficiently dissolved in 10 mL DI water. Subsequently, the transparent solution was transferred into a 30 mL Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 200℃ for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was filtered through a microfiltration membrane (Nylon-66, 220 nm), and then dialyzed against DI water using a dialysis membrane for 48 h. Finally, the red-brown aqueous solution was precipitated and rinsed with ethanol, and vacuum-dried at 30℃ to collect CDs powder.

The F-CDs were synthesized according to the literature with slight modifications [26]. 50 mg CDs and 25 mg FITC were dispersed in 5 mL of triethylamine aqueous solution (pH = 8 ∼ 9) under an N2 atmosphere and stirred at room temperature for 48 h in the dark. The result solution was dialyzed against DI water for another 48 h to remove the unbound FITC. Finally, the amorphous solid powder of the F-CDs was collected by lyophilization. For testing its optical properties, 150 μL (2 mg/mL) of F-CDs solutions were mixed with 2.85 mL of the ammonia solutions. Then, the fluorescence spectra (λex = 280–380 nm) of the resulting solutions were recorded as soon as possible.

The ratiometric nanofiber-based sensor (RNS) was fabricated by electrospinning. Firstly, 50 μL stock solution (30 mg F-CDs in 1.5 mL water) was added to 1 g 20 wt% polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, average Mw 1300000) aqueous solution for preparation of the spinning liquid. Such liquid was then put into a syringe equipped with a single nozzle (0.3 mm). Under the action of 20 kV voltage, the solution underwent the processes of stretching, solvent evaporation, and curing, eventually forming continuous ultra-fine nanofibers on an aluminum plate. The fabricated RNS was obtained after electrospinning for 20 min at 35 °C. Besides, the effect of different F-CDs concentrations in PVP nanofibers on the sensing capacity was recorded in Fig. S1.

The NH3 vapor was produced by the evaporation of the aqueous ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) solution, as shown in the Supplementary Material. Before the test began, the humidity in the test chamber was adjusted by moistening or drying N2, followed by the injection of 20 mL of NH3 vapor with different concentrations (equilibration time: 10 s). The fluorescent spectra of the samples were recorded under an excitation of 340 nm, while the fluorescent images of samples were taken under an excitation of 365 nm.

A portable ammoniemia detector was constructed using four poly(tetrafluoroethylene) plates (PTFE, 7.5 cm × 2.5 cm × 0.1 cm) with different shapes to simulate the blood ammonia measurement (a picture of this detector was given in the later section of 3.4). The gaps between layers were sealed by the light-curing adhesive. Firstly, the simulated blood ammonia (100 μL) was prepared by adding different concentrations of NH4OH to synthetic blood (containing amaranth dye, thickener, water, et al., Phygene Biotechnology Co., LTD, China), and then injected into the sample cell. A glass was fixed on the PTFE plates to facilitate the transfer of NH3 vapor from blood through the gas channel to the sensing chamber containing the RNS (1 cm × 1 cm). Finally, the fluorescent images of the detector were recorded by a smartphone under an excitation of 365 nm, and the color characteristics were obtained using Adobe Photoshop 2021.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the CDs were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method using anhydrous citric acid and 1,2-ethanediamine as the carbon source and surface passivation agent, respectively. The DLS analysis revealed that the average diameter of the CDs distributed in water is 2.91 nm (Fig. 1 a). A similar result could also be obtained from the cross-sectional view of CDs powder through the AFM characterization.

Fig. 1b, C, N and O elements were predominant in CDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum indicated several peaks, consisting of C = C, C-N, C-O, and C = O at 284.7, 284.9, 286.0, 287.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 1c) [27]. Besides, the decomposition of N1s spectrum exhibited three peaks at 398.2, 398.7, and 400.1 eV, which were attributed to N-H, N-(C3) and N = H, respectively (Fig. 1d). These observations indicated that the as-prepared CDs were rich with amino groups on the surface. Such amino groups were beneficial for further chemical modification on the surface of nanoparticles, which has been demonstrated in many previous studies [28,29]. As shown in Fig. 1e, a ratiometric fluorescent probe (F-CDs) was prepared by reacting the amino group on the surface of CDs with the isothiocyanate group in FITC via the nucleophilic addition reaction to form thiourea. The S 2p appeared in the XPS spectrum of F-CDs, which could be divided into S 2p3/2 (160.0 eV) and S 2p1/2 (161.3 eV). The C = S band could also be found at 285.6 eV in the C 1s spectrum of F-CDs [30]. FTIR measurement additionally confirmed the functional groups of CDs and F-CDs, and the result was provided in Fig. 1f. The newly appeared absorption peak at 1211 cm−1 (νC=S) and the disappeared absorption peak at 2104 cm−1 (νN=C=S) could demonstrate that the FITC was conjugated onto the surface of CDs [31,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fret-based ratiometric nanoprobe determination of ammonia solutions
ce:section.ce:para:
As demonstrated in Fig. S2a, the synthetic CDs have a typical carbon dot feature, i.e., tunable emission. With the excitation wavelengths moving from 280 to 380 nm, the emission peaks of CDs red shifted obviously, while their fluorescence intensity showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. The quantum yield (φ) of CDs in water was determined to be 0.18 at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm using quinine sulfate as a standard (φref = 0.54), as shown in Fig. S3. Notably, there was a high degree of overlap between the emission band of CDs and the absorption band of FITC (Fig. S2b). Considering the formation of the thiourea band, CDs and FITC have the potential to construct the FRET system in the F-CDs nanoprobe, and the calculated φ value of F-CDs was only decreased to 0.12. Since the excitation-dependent emission of CDs could be tuned to match the absorption of FITC, the emission band of FITC (second emission peak in Fig. S4) centered at 525 nm gradually increased as the excitation wavelength increased. Such a simultaneous adjustment phenomenon has been demonstrated to help maximize FRET. Besides, the conjugation efficiency of FITC in F-CDs was estimated to be 32.57% by spectrophotometry (Fig. S5), i.e., one-third of the CDs complete the conjugation reaction with FITC.
(1)
N
H 4
O H →
N H
4
+
+
O H
-

The sensing properties of the prepared F-CDs were further investigated (Fig. 2 a) by dispersing them in different ammonia aqueous solutions (0–600 μM). In the initial condition, CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence at 435 nm, while the molecular structure of FITC remained as a non-conjugated lactone tautomer with weak fluorescence intensity at 525 nm. Upon mixing ammonia solution, NH3 in aqueous solution reacted as a base to ammonium ions (
N H
4
+
) and hydroxide ions (
O H
-
) according to Eq.1. The basic condition turned the molecular structure of FITC into a conjugated dianionic tautomer, thus emitting the intense green fluorescence (Fig. S6) [33]. The bathochromic behavior of fluorescence spectra could be used to control the ratiometric sensing system. As shown in Fig. 2b, a calibration graph was drawn with IFITC/ICDs (where IFITC and ICDs were the fluorescence intensities belonging to FITC and CDs in the fluorescence spectra of F-CDs, respectively) as the ordinate and NH3 concentration along the abscissa. The sigmoidal curve was well fitted using the Boltzmann equation. Besides, the ratiometric sensing process also showed a naked eye-visible detection property under the excitation of 365 nm. In Fig. 2c, the fluorescence color coordinates of F-CDs on the CIE chromaticity diagram were shifted from blue (0.17, 0.16) to green (0.25, 0.50).

The time-resolved fluorescent measurement was further performed to record the fluorescence decay of F-CDs in ammonia solution. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) (Fig. S7) of F-CDs was calculated using a double-exponential model with an average value of 8.14 ns. The emission decay consisted of two processes due to electronic transitions in the F-CDs: the short-lived component τ1 (1.63 ns) and the long-lived component τ2 (9.32 ns), revealing a substantial connection between the core and surface states of F-CDs. Instead, the τ value substantially decreased to 5.56 ns with the addition of 600 μM ammonia solution, implying the existence of FRET between (donor) and FITC (acceptor) [24,34]. The process of FRET descripted in Fig. S8. To illustrate the FRET process in detail, we calculated the energy transfer efficiency (E) and the average distance between the donor and the acceptor (Fig. S9). Simply speaking, the value of E was calculated by comparing the fluorescence intensity of F-CDs at 435 nm before and after the addition of 600 μM ammonia solution, where Ro is the Förster radius and R is the average distance between the donor and the acceptor (Eq.2) [35]. Benefiting from good spectral overlap, the E of F-CDs was up to ca. 57.4%, and the value of R could be further obtained as ca. 3.07 nm with a R0 value of ca. 3.15 nm. Overall, the F-CDs nanoprobe with the FRET protocol could create dual-chromatic emissions with a single-wavelength excitation, offering more effective and convenient ratiometric detection than single-emission systems or multi-colored systems that need several excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the fabricated ratiometric sensors (RNS)
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 3 a, the synthetic dual-emission nanoprobe with a blue initial fluorescence could be fixed in the PVP-based nanofibrous membrane by electrospinning. The microscopic images of the fabricated membrane confirmed that smooth, uniform, and continuous nanofibers were formed with a mean diameter of ca. 400 nm (Fig. 3b and S10). The potential of the membrane as a ratiometric sensor for tracking gaseous ammonia was subsequently explored.
(3)
N
H 4
O H
a q
→
N H
3
g
+
H 2
O
( a q )

Ammonia analytes produced in medical diagnosis are mostly moist [36]. Thus, NH3 vapor, which was produced by the evaporation of the aqueous NH4OH solution according to Eq. (3), was a good testing gas [37]. The RNS was first placed in the sensing chamber filled with 20–90% relative humidity (RH) air and then exposed to 300 parts per million (ppm) NH3 vapor (Fig. S11a). It was found that the fluorescence intensity ratios (IFITC/ICDs ) of the ratiometric sensor were stable in a dry/humid environment. However, the sensitivity of RNS to NH3 vapor was significantly increased under high humidity conditions, which might be attributed to the good water solubility of NH3. In detail, NH3 (gaseous phase) could combine with water molecules to form a larger ammonia molecule, and the PVP nanofibers with good hygroscopic properties could adsorb water molecules in the test chamber [38]. Strong hydrogen bonds were formed between water molecules and NH3 molecules, thus enhancing the amount of NH3 absorbed by the RNS sensor. Based on the mechanism, RNS exhibited unparalleled NH3-sensing behavior in a humid environment. In contrast, the optical performance of RNS was not affected by temperature or excitation time (Fig. S11b, c). Thus, subsequent experiments were conducted at 25 °C and 90% RH. In Fig. S11d, we chose five kinds of water-soluble gases (including acetone, H2O2, EtOH, O2, and CO2) as interfering substances to further investigate the NH3-sensing behavior of RNS. Results showed that the fluorescence intensity ratios of the fabricated RNS did not change upon exposure to these interfering gases. Nevertheless, the ratios increased significantly when 300 ppm NH3 vapor was injected into the test chamber. Therefore, the designed ratiometric sensor could selectively identify NH3 with little interference from other gases.

To further explore the response characteristics of the fabricated sensor, F-CDs’ fluorescence spectra after exposure to different concentrations of NH3 were recorded in Fig. 3c. During the sensing process, the value of IFITC/ICDs was linearly proportional to the NH3 concentration range of 0 ppm to 300 ppm, as seen in Fig. 3d. Based on the calibration equations for fitting lines, it was expected to quantitatively detect NH3 concentration in confined spaces. The limit of detection (LOD) determined by 3 × ε/k was 0.53 ppm under 90% RH [13], where k was the slope of the fit line (Y1) and ε was the standard deviation for the blank signal. Besides, the response time (τ90%) of the ratiometric sensor, i.e., the time for the IFITC/ICDs value reaching to 90% of its terminal state upon exposure to 300 ppm NH3, was ca. 4.3 s (Fig. 3e and Movie S1). More importantly, the response of the sensor to gaseous NH3 was reversible. After re-exposure to fresh air, the fluorescence intensity of FITC was quenched to its original state, accompanied by a return of the fluorescent color to blue, as shown in Fig. 3f. Such a cycle sensing test could be performed more than 5 times, proving that the fabricated fluorescent sensor has good NH3 responsiveness and high stability. Furthermore, due to the excellent dispersion in water, F-CDs nanoprobe could also be fixed on glass, polystyrene panel, and paper by spraying, coating, and printing, respectively (Fig. S12a-d). And the sensing properties of F-CDs in different nanofibers were also recorded in Fig. S12e. Compared with the reported NH3 sensors (Table S3), RNS showed excellent NH3 sensing performance and stability, with potential applications in medical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, smart packaging, etc. [16,39,40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prototyped applications for the ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: For an easy and practicable use of the sensing films, we designed a portable ammoniemia detector (PAD), similar to a glucometer, based on the color change of ratiometric upon exposure to NH3 vapor, which was expected to streamline the existing blood ammonia testing process. The structure of PAD was illustrated in Fig. 4c, which could minimize the interference of NH3 adsorption on the internal surfaces. Simulated blood samples could be stored directly into the sample cell without further processing, while the RNS was installed on the other side of the PAD to monitor the NH3 concentration that was volatilized from the blood. It could be seen that the ammoniated artificial blood was able to change the fluorescent color of RNS from blue to green (Fig. 4d). To our delight, the G/B values (G: green channel, B: blue channel) obtained by the color recognition were synchronous with the increase of NH3 concentration in artificial blood (Fig. 4e). Even though a direct relationship between blood and breath ammonia concentrations needs more demonstrations, the fabricated ratiometric sensor is expected to be a valid and feasible tool for the establishment of the link.

In summary, a nanoprobe based on CDs and FITC complexes was successfully synthesized to efficiently detect ammonia concentrations in the solution. Based on the FRET protocol, the dual-emission nanoprobe achieved blue-green conversion of the fluorescent color under a single-wavelength excitation upon exposure to NH3. Besides, the nanoprobe could be fixed on PVP nanofibers by electrospinning to obtain a ratiometric fluorescent sensing film. Results showed that RNS possessed high sensitivity, obvious color change, rapid response, and excellent reversibility for 0–300 ppm gaseous NH3, and its theoretical detection limit was 0.53 ppm. Subsequently, the fabricated sensing film was used to detect trace ammonia in human breath and blood. Although the prototyped applications are in their infancy, the presented ratiometric sensor is expected to be a simple and low-cost tool for meeting the needs of ammonia concentration detection in the human body during early medical diagnosis.

Shaoyong Cai: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Mengyu Deng: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Dongyan Zhou: Data curation. Fengyu Su: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yan Jun Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation. Yanqing Tian: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 21774054) and the start-up fund of SUSTech (Y01256114).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109176.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of total ammonia nitrogen (TAN), nitrate and total organic carbon (TOC) from aquaculture wastewater using electrochemical technology: a review
Desalination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mook, W. T.; Chakrabarti, M. H.; Aroua, M. K.; Khan, G. M. A.; Ali, B. S.; Islam, M. S.; Abu Hassan, M. A. Removal of total ammonia nitrogen (TAN), nitrate and total organic carbon (TOC) from aquaculture wastewater using electrochemical technology: A review. Desalination 2012, 285, 1-13, Review. Int. 2022, 9, Article; Early Access. doi: 10.1007/s12072-022-10418-4.
